seo.title: Chiang Mai Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Everything about Chiang Mai Province: Doi Suthep, Doi Inthanon, Old City temples, night markets, and 25 districts to explore. Area: 20,107 km², population: 1.9 million. Plan your visit.

overview: Chiang Mai Province is the largest province in northern Thailand and the country's second most important cultural center after Bangkok. Covering over 20,000 km² of mountainous terrain, it was the heart of the ancient Lanna Kingdom founded in 1296 by King Mengrai. The province encompasses Thailand's highest peak (Doi Inthanon at 2,565 meters), lush river valleys along the Ping River, and hill tribe communities preserving centuries-old traditions. Its capital, Chiang Mai city, is a walled old city ringed by a moat, home to over 300 Buddhist temples, world-class street food, and a thriving digital nomad scene — all set against a dramatic mountain backdrop.
geography: Chiang Mai Province covers 20,107 km² of predominantly mountainous terrain in northern Thailand, bordered by Myanmar (Shan State) to the north. The Thanon Thong Chai Range and Daen Lao Range form the western and eastern boundaries, while the Ping River — one of the major tributaries of the Chao Phraya — flows through the central valley where the capital city sits at roughly 300 meters elevation. Doi Inthanon (2,565 m) in the southwestern part is the highest point in all of Thailand. The province features dense tropical forests, terraced rice paddies in the valleys, and a cooler climate than central Thailand, with winter temperatures occasionally dropping below 10°C in the highlands.
highlights: Wat Phra That Doi Suthep — the iconic golden temple perched on the mountain overlooking the city, visible from across Chiang Mai
Doi Inthanon National Park — Thailand's highest peak at 2,565 meters with twin royal pagodas, waterfalls, and cloud forests
Chiang Mai Old City — a 700-year-old walled square with moat, over 30 temples within walking distance
Sunday Walking Street Market — the region's largest night market stretching 1 km along Ratchadamnoen Road with handicrafts, art, and street food
Ethical elephant sanctuaries — interact with rescued elephants at responsible parks in the Mae Taeng valley
Thai cooking classes — Chiang Mai is Thailand's cooking class capital, with dozens of schools offering market-to-table experiences
Chiang Dao Cave and mountain — a 12 km cave system and the province's third-highest peak for trekking
Yi Peng Lantern Festival — thousands of sky lanterns released during the November full moon, one of Thailand's most magical events

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Nov–Feb: Cool season with temperatures of 15–28°C in the city, colder in the mountains. Clear skies, the Yi Peng Lantern Festival in November, and the Flower Festival in February. Peak tourist season with highest hotel prices.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Mar–May: Hot season reaching 35–40°C in the valley. March–April sees the burning season with smoky haze from agricultural fires — air quality can be poor. Songkran (April 13–15) is celebrated with massive water fights throughout the Old City.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Jun–Oct: Rainy season with afternoon showers, but mornings are usually clear. The landscape is lush and green. Hotel prices drop 30–50%. September–October sees occasional flooding in low-lying areas.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit November to February for the best weather and festivals. Avoid March–April if you're sensitive to air quality. The rainy season (June–October) offers great value and fewer crowds, with rain typically limited to 1–2 hour afternoon bursts.

topAttractions.name: Wat Phra That Doi Suthep
topAttractions.description: Chiang Mai's most sacred temple, perched at 1,055 meters on the mountainside west of the city. A 306-step naga staircase leads to the glittering golden chedi housing a relic of the Buddha. The terrace offers panoramic views of the city and the Ping River valley. Founded in 1383 during the Lanna Kingdom.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 30 THB (~$1 USD) for foreigners. Cable car/tram up the steps: 50 THB

topAttractions.name: Doi Inthanon National Park
topAttractions.description: Home to Thailand's highest peak at 2,565 meters. The park features the stunning twin Royal Pagodas (Naphamethinidon and Naphaphonphumisiri), the Kew Mae Pan nature trail through cloud forest, and several waterfalls including Wachirathan and Mae Klang. Hill tribe villages dot the lower slopes.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 300 THB (~$8.50 USD) for foreigners, 150 THB for children. Vehicle fee: 30 THB for motorcycles, 50 THB for cars

topAttractions.name: Wat Chedi Luang
topAttractions.description: A massive 14th-century temple in the heart of the Old City, originally built in 1391. The enormous ruined chedi once stood 82 meters tall — an earthquake in 1545 reduced it to its current 60 meters. The city pillar (Lak Mueang) sits within the compound. Monk chats are available daily.

topAttractions.name: Wat Phra Singh
topAttractions.description: One of Chiang Mai's most revered temples, built in 1345 to house the ashes of King Kham Fu. The Lai Kham chapel contains the Phra Phuttha Sihing Buddha, a highly venerated image. The temple showcases classic Lanna architecture with intricate wood carvings and gilded decorations.

topAttractions.name: Lanna Folklife Museum
topAttractions.description: Housed in a beautiful 1935 colonial-style building on Prapokklao Road, this museum brings Lanna culture to life through interactive exhibits on northern Thai traditions, textiles, religion, and daily life. Covers everything from hill tribe customs to traditional Lanna architecture.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 90 THB (~$2.50 USD) for adults, 40 THB for children

topAttractions.name: Chiang Dao Cave (Tham Chiang Dao)
topAttractions.description: A dramatic limestone cave system extending over 12 km into the mountain, though only a few hundred meters are accessible to visitors. The illuminated sections contain Buddha images, stalactites, and crystal formations. Located at the base of Doi Chiang Dao (2,175 m), the province's third-highest peak.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 40 THB (~$1 USD). Guided tour into unlit sections with lantern: 200 THB

topAttractions.name: Elephant Nature Park
topAttractions.description: Thailand's most well-known ethical elephant sanctuary, founded by Lek Chailert in the Mae Taeng valley. Home to over 80 rescued elephants living in natural surroundings. Visitors observe, feed, and bathe elephants — no riding allowed. Full-day and half-day programs include vegetarian lunch.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 2,500 THB (~$70 USD) for full-day visit including transport, lunch, and guide

topAttractions.name: Sunday Walking Street (Ratchadamnoen Road)
topAttractions.description: Chiang Mai's most famous market transforms the Ratchadamnoen Road in the Old City into a 1 km pedestrian zone every Sunday from 4 PM to midnight. Local artisans sell handmade crafts, Lanna textiles, silver jewelry, and hill tribe artwork. Street food stalls line every side street. The Saturday version runs on Wua Lai Road.

localFood: Khao Soi — Chiang Mai's signature dish: egg noodles in a rich coconut curry broth topped with crispy fried noodles, served with pickled mustard greens and shallots
Sai Ua (Chiang Mai sausage) — spicy northern Thai sausage made with red curry paste, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, and galangal, grilled over charcoal
Nam Prik Ong — a northern Thai chili dip made with minced pork, tomatoes, and dried chilies, served with crispy pork rinds and fresh vegetables
Nam Prik Noom — a smoky green chili dip made from roasted young green chilies, garlic, and shallots — a Lanna staple
Kanom Jeen Nam Ngiao — rice noodles in a spicy tomato-pork broth with blood cakes and fermented bean paste, a Tai Yai-influenced dish
Khaep Mu (crispy pork rinds) — freshly fried pork skin, a ubiquitous northern Thai snack eaten with nam prik dips
Laab Mueang — northern-style minced meat salad with dried spice mix (phrik larp) including long pepper and makhwen (Sichuan pepper)
Khao Kha Mu — slow-braised pork leg over rice with pickled mustard greens and a hard-boiled egg, a beloved street food staple
culture: Chiang Mai was the capital of the Lanna Kingdom (1296–1558), and Lanna culture remains distinctly different from central Thai traditions. The province preserves its own script (Tai Tham), architectural style (multi-tiered roofs with elaborate woodcarving), and cuisine (heavily influenced by Burmese and Shan flavors). Over 300 Buddhist temples in the city alone showcase Lanna art, and the province is home to multiple hill tribes — Karen, Hmong, Lahu, Akha, and Lisu — each maintaining their own languages and customs. Major cultural events include Yi Peng (sky lantern festival), Loy Krathong (floating lantern ceremony), Songkran (Thai New Year), and the Chiang Mai Flower Festival in February.
gettingThere: From Bangkok, flights to Chiang Mai International Airport (CNX) take 1 hour 10 minutes, with 30+ daily flights on Thai AirAsia, Nok Air, Thai Lion Air, Thai Smile, and Bangkok Airways. Prices range from 800–3,000 THB one-way. The overnight sleeper train from Bangkok's Bang Sue Grand Station takes 11–13 hours (lower berth second class: ~800 THB, first class private cabin: ~1,500 THB). VIP buses from Mo Chit terminal take 9–10 hours (~500–700 THB). The airport is just 3 km southwest of the Old City — a taxi or Grab ride costs 100–150 THB.
gettingAround: Within Chiang Mai city, red songthaews (shared pickup trucks) are the main public transport — flag one down and negotiate the fare (20–40 THB per person for short trips within the city). Grab and Bolt ride-hailing apps work well and are the most convenient option (50–150 THB for most city trips). Renting a motorbike is popular (200–300 THB/day) but drive carefully — traffic can be chaotic. The Old City is compact enough to explore on foot or by bicycle (50–100 THB/day rental). For day trips to Doi Inthanon or Chiang Dao, hire a car with driver (~1,500–2,500 THB/day) or join a group tour.
travelTips: Avoid visiting March–April if you have respiratory issues — agricultural burning creates heavy smog (AQI often exceeds 200)
Dress modestly when visiting temples — cover shoulders and knees. Most temples provide sarongs for rent or free
The Old City moat area is walkable — stay inside or near the moat for easy access to temples, restaurants, and markets
Download the Grab app before arriving — it's cheaper and more reliable than negotiating with tuk-tuk drivers
Book elephant sanctuaries directly through their websites — avoid street touts and tour agencies that mark up prices 50–100%
The Nimman (Nimmanhaemin) area is the trendy neighborhood for cafes, boutique shopping, and nightlife — popular with digital nomads
Carry cash for temples and small vendors — card acceptance is limited outside hotels and malls
Visit Doi Suthep early morning (before 9 AM) to avoid crowds and catch the best views before afternoon haze
